preamble.summary: Proverbs 21 continues the Solomonic collection with proverbs emphasizing divine sovereignty over human plans, the priority of justice over ritual, and the futility of resisting the LORD's purposes. The chapter opens with the striking image of the king's heart as a water channel in God's hand and closes with the declaration that no wisdom or strategy can stand against the LORD.
preamble.remarkable: This chapter contains one of the most politically radical statements in ancient Near Eastern literature: the king's heart is merely an irrigation ditch that God directs wherever He pleases (v1). In a world where kings claimed divine status or divine favor as their right, this proverb reduces royal authority to passive clay. Verse 3 elevates justice and righteousness above sacrifice — a theme that echoes the prophets (Isaiah 1:11-17, Amos 5:21-24, Micah 6:6-8) — suggesting that the wisdom tradition and the prophetic tradition share this conviction independently. The chapter's final verse (v31) — 'The horse is prepared for the day of battle, but victory belongs to the LORD' — is a perfect summary of biblical theology: human preparation is legitimate, but outcomes belong to God alone.
preamble.friction: Verse 9 ('Better to live on a corner of the roof than share a house with a quarrelsome woman') reflects the patriarchal social structure where men controlled domestic space. The observation is pragmatic, not prescriptive, but it addresses only men's frustration without acknowledging women's perspective. Verse 18 ('The wicked becomes a ransom for the righteous') does not teach substitutionary atonement but observes that the consequences intended for the righteous often fall on the wicked instead — a reversal pattern, not a transaction.
preamble.connections: The 'king's heart as water channels' image (v1) connects to Isaiah 44:27-28, where God directs Cyrus as His shepherd. The priority of justice over sacrifice (v3) parallels 1 Samuel 15:22 and Hosea 6:6. The 'horse prepared for battle' (v31) echoes Psalm 20:7 ('Some trust in chariots and some in horses, but we invoke the name of the LORD our God'). The chapter's emphasis on divine sovereignty over human plans links to Proverbs 16:1, 9 and 19:21.

verses.text_hebrew: פַּלְגֵי־מַ֣יִם לֶב־מֶ֭לֶךְ בְּיַד־יְהוָ֑ה עַֽל־כָּל־אֲשֶׁ֖ר יַחְפֹּ֣ץ יַטֶּֽנּוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: The king's heart is in the hand of the LORD, as the rivers of water: he turneth it whithersoever he will.
verses.rendering:
The king's heart is a water channel in the LORD's hand —
He directs it wherever He pleases.
verses.expanded_rendering: The image is agricultural: palge mayim are irrigation ditches that a farmer opens and closes to direct water flow across a field. The king — the most powerful human figure in the ancient world — is reduced to a watercourse that God steers at will. The verb yattenu ('he turns it, he inclines it') suggests gentle, constant guidance rather than violent coercion. God does not break the king's will; He redirects it, the way a farmer redirects water by shifting a small dam of earth.
translator_notes: Palge mayim (channels of water) is the same phrase used in Psalm 1:3 for the streams beside which the righteous tree is planted. Here the metaphor shifts from nourishment to control.
The proverb does not say the king is aware of God's direction. The water does not know it is being redirected. This is a statement about God's sovereignty, not about the king's piety.

key_terms.semantic_range: irrigation ditches, water courses, channels; divisions of water for agricultural use
key_terms.note: These are not rivers or streams but constructed channels that a farmer controls. The metaphor implies that God manages royal power the way a farmer manages water distribution.

verses.text_hebrew: כָּל־דֶּ֣רֶךְ אִ֭ישׁ יָשָׁ֣ר בְּעֵינָ֑יו וְתֹכֵ֖ן לִבּ֣וֹת יְהוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Every way of a man is right in his own eyes: but the LORD pondereth the hearts.
verses.rendering:
Every path a person takes looks right to him,
but the LORD weighs hearts.
translator_notes: The verb tokhen ('weighs, examines, measures') comes from the world of scales and balances. God does not merely observe — He assesses with precision. The same verb appears in Proverbs 16:2.

key_terms.semantic_range: to weigh, measure, assess, examine; to test by standard
key_terms.note: From the language of commerce: placing something on a scale to determine its true weight. God measures the heart against a standard the person cannot see.

verses.text_hebrew: עֲ֭שֹׂה צְדָקָ֣ה וּמִשְׁפָּ֑ט נִבְחָ֖ר לַיהוָ֣ה מִזָּֽבַח׃
verses.text_kjv: To do justice and judgment is more acceptable to the LORD than sacrifice.
verses.rendering:
Practicing righteousness and justice
is preferred by the LORD over sacrifice.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is one of the wisdom tradition's clearest statements that ethical conduct outranks ritual performance. Tsedaqah (righteousness — right relationship, right action) and mishpat (justice — fair judgment, equitable treatment) together represent the full scope of moral obligation. The word nivchar ('chosen, preferred') does not abolish sacrifice but establishes a hierarchy: when forced to choose between ritual and ethics, God chooses ethics. This parallels the prophetic critique in 1 Samuel 15:22, Isaiah 1:11-17, and Hosea 6:6.
translator_notes: The pairing of tsedaqah and mishpat is one of the most important word pairs in the Hebrew Bible. Together they describe a society that functions as God intended — where relationships are right and judgments are fair.

key_terms.semantic_range: righteousness, justice, right conduct, covenant faithfulness; doing what is right in relationship
key_terms.note: Tsedaqah is relational, not merely legal. A tsaddiq is a person who fulfills the obligations of every relationship — to God, to family, to neighbor, to the vulnerable.

key_terms.semantic_range: justice, judgment, ordinance, legal decision; the act of rendering fair verdicts and protecting rights
key_terms.note: Mishpat encompasses both the process of judging and the content of just decisions. It includes defending the powerless and holding the powerful accountable.

verses.text_hebrew: רוּם־עֵ֭ינַיִם וּרְחַב־לֵ֑ב נִ֖ר רְשָׁעִ֣ים חַטָּֽאת׃
verses.text_kjv: An high look, and a proud heart, and the plowing of the wicked, is sin.
verses.rendering:
Haughty eyes and an inflated heart —
the lamp of the wicked is sin.
translator_notes: The Hebrew nir can mean either 'lamp' or 'freshly plowed land.' If 'lamp,' the proverb says that what lights the wicked person's way is sin itself — their guiding light is darkness. If 'plowed land,' it means that everything the wicked produce is sin. Both readings yield the same conclusion: arrogance pervades every aspect of the wicked person's life.

key_terms.semantic_range: lamp, light; or freshly plowed ground, tillage
key_terms.note: The ambiguity is ancient and may be intentional. A lamp suggests guidance and direction; plowed land suggests productivity and effort. Either way, what the wicked produce or follow is sin.

verses.text_hebrew: מַחְשְׁב֣וֹת חָ֭רוּץ אַךְ־לְמוֹתָ֑ר וְכָל־אָ֝֗ץ אַךְ־לְמַחְסֽוֹר׃
verses.text_kjv: The thoughts of the diligent tend only to plenteousness; but of every one that is hasty only to want.
verses.rendering:
The plans of the diligent lead only to abundance,
but everyone who rushes ends only in lack.
translator_notes: The contrast is between charutz ('diligent, sharp, decisive') and ats ('to hurry, to be hasty'). Diligence is not slowness but purposeful planning followed by steady execution. Haste is action without thought — the illusion of productivity.

verses.text_hebrew: פֹּ֣עַל א֭וֹצָרוֹת בִּלְשׁ֣וֹן שָׁ֑קֶר הֶ֥בֶל נִ֝דָּ֗ף מְבַקְשֵׁי־מָֽוֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: The getting of treasures by a lying tongue is a vanity tossed to and fro of them that seek death.
verses.rendering:
Gaining wealth by a lying tongue
is a fleeting vapor — those who chase it chase death.
translator_notes: Hevel niddaf ('vapor driven, mist blown away') uses the same hevel that dominates Ecclesiastes — breath, vapor, something that vanishes instantly. Wealth gained through deception is not merely unstable; it is already gone the moment you grasp it.

key_terms.semantic_range: breath, vapor, mist, vanity, futility; something without substance or permanence
key_terms.note: Hevel is the signature word of Ecclesiastes but appears throughout wisdom literature. It names the condition of things that look substantial but dissolve on contact.

verses.text_hebrew: שֹׁד־רְשָׁעִ֥ים יְגוֹרֵ֑ם כִּ֥י מֵ֝אֲנ֗וּ לַעֲשׂ֥וֹת מִשְׁפָּֽט׃
verses.text_kjv: The robbery of the wicked shall destroy them; because they refuse to do judgment.
verses.rendering:
The violence of the wicked drags them away,
because they refuse to practice justice.
translator_notes: Shod ('violence, destruction, robbery') becomes the agent that devours its own practitioners. The verb yegovrem ('drags them, sweeps them away') pictures violence as a current that carries the violent person downstream to ruin.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲפַכְפַּ֣ךְ דֶּ֣רֶךְ אִ֣ישׁ וָזָ֑ר וְ֝זַ֗ךְ יָשָׁ֥ר פָּעֳלֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: The way of man is froward and strange: but as for the pure, his work is right.
verses.rendering:
Crooked is the path of a guilty person,
but the conduct of the pure is upright.
translator_notes: Hafakhpakh ('twisted, crooked, perverse') is an intensified form suggesting not just one wrong turn but a path that twists back on itself repeatedly. The contrast with zakh ('pure, clean, transparent') and yashar ('straight, upright') emphasizes that moral clarity produces directional clarity.

verses.text_hebrew: ט֗וֹב לָשֶׁ֥בֶת עַל־פִּנַּת־גָּ֑ג מֵאֵ֥שֶׁת מִ֝דְיָנִ֗ים וּבֵ֥ית חָבֶֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: It is better to dwell in a corner of the housetop, than with a brawling woman and in a wide house.
verses.rendering:
Better to sit on a corner of the roof
than to share a house with a quarrelsome woman.
translator_notes: The flat rooftop was exposed to wind, rain, and sun — the worst living space in an Israelite home. The proverb uses 'better...than' (tov...min) format to make a deliberately absurd comparison: even the most uncomfortable solitude is preferable to relentless domestic conflict. The observation is repeated in 25:24. The Hebrew eshet midyanim ('woman of contentions') targets behavior, not gender, though the male audience of the collection means the examples address men's domestic experience.

verses.text_hebrew: נֶ֣פֶשׁ רָ֭שָׁע אִוְּתָה־רָ֑ע לֹא־יֻחַ֖ן בְּעֵינָ֣יו רֵעֵֽהוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: The soul of the wicked desireth evil: his neighbour findeth no favour in his eyes.
verses.rendering:
The appetite of the wicked craves what is harmful;
his neighbor finds no mercy in his eyes.
translator_notes: Nefesh here means 'appetite, desire, inner drive' rather than 'soul' in the Greek philosophical sense. The wicked person's fundamental orientation is toward harm — evil is not an occasional lapse but a craving. The result is social: when desire is directed toward harm, the neighbor becomes an obstacle rather than a fellow human deserving compassion.

verses.text_hebrew: בַּעְנֹ֣שׁ לֵ֭ץ יֶחְכַּם־פֶּ֑תִי וּבְהַשְׂכִּ֥יל לְ֝חָכָ֗ם יִקַּח־דָּֽעַת׃
verses.text_kjv: When the scorner is punished, the simple is made wise: and when the wise is instructed, he receiveth knowledge.
verses.rendering:
When a scoffer is punished, the naive become wise;
when a wise person is instructed, he gains knowledge.
translator_notes: The proverb identifies two learning methods: the naive (peti) learn from watching consequences fall on others, while the wise (chakham) learn directly from instruction. The scoffer (lets) learns from neither — he exists in the proverb only as a negative example for others. Three character types, three relationships to learning.

verses.text_hebrew: מַשְׂכִּ֣יל צַ֭דִּיק לְבֵ֣ית רָשָׁ֑ע מְסַלֵּ֖ף רְשָׁעִ֣ים לָרָֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: The righteous man wisely considereth the house of the wicked: but God overthroweth the wicked for their wickedness.
verses.rendering:
The Righteous One observes the house of the wicked;
He overturns the wicked to ruin.
verses.expanded_rendering: The tsaddiq in this verse is debated: it may be a righteous person who shrewdly observes the wicked household's trajectory, or it may be God Himself — the Righteous One — who watches and then topples the wicked. The verb mesallef ('overturns, subverts, ruins') suggests active intervention, which favors reading the subject as God. If so, the proverb pairs divine observation with divine action: God sees and then acts.
translator_notes: The capitalized 'Righteous One' reflects the interpretive possibility that God is the subject. The ambiguity may be intentional — both the righteous person and God observe the wicked household, and both know its end.

key_terms.semantic_range: righteous, just, innocent, in the right; one who conforms to the standard of covenant relationship
key_terms.note: When applied to God, tsaddiq means the one who defines and embodies the standard. When applied to humans, it means one who lives according to that standard.

verses.text_hebrew: אֹטֵ֣ם אָ֭זְנוֹ מִזַּעֲקַ֣ת דָּ֑ל גַּֽם־ה֥וּא יִ֝קְרָ֗א וְלֹ֣א יֵעָנֶֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Whoso stoppeth his ears at the cry of the poor, he also shall cry himself, but shall not be heard.
verses.rendering:
Whoever blocks his ear from the cry of the poor —
he too will cry out and not be answered.
translator_notes: The reciprocity is precise: the verb za'aq ('to cry out, to shout for help') applies to both the poor person and the one who ignored him. The one who refused to hear will find himself unheard. This is not abstract karma but covenant logic — God responds to those who respond to others.

verses.text_hebrew: מַתָּ֣ן בַּ֭סֵּתֶר יִכְפֶּה־אָ֑ף וְשֹׁ֥חַד בַּ֝חֵ֗ק חֵמָ֥ה עַזָּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: A gift in secret pacifieth anger: and a reward in the bosom strong wrath.
verses.rendering:
A gift given in secret calms anger,
and a concealed bribe subdues fierce rage.
translator_notes: The proverb observes a social reality without endorsing it: gifts and bribes work. The distinction between mattan ('gift') and shochad ('bribe') is significant — the first is neutral, the second carries negative moral weight. The proverb is descriptive, not prescriptive; it tells you how the world works, not how it should work.

verses.text_hebrew: שִׂמְחָ֣ה לַ֭צַּדִּיק עֲשׂ֣וֹת מִשְׁפָּ֑ט וּ֝מְחִתָּ֗ה לְפֹ֣עֲלֵי אָֽוֶן׃
verses.text_kjv: It is joy to the just to do judgment: but destruction shall be to the workers of iniquity.
verses.rendering:
Doing justice is a joy to the righteous
but a terror to those who practice wickedness.
translator_notes: The contrast is revealing: the same action — the administration of justice — produces opposite emotional responses depending on the person's character. The righteous find simchah ('joy, gladness') in fair judgment; the wicked find mechittah ('terror, ruin, destruction'). Justice is a mirror that reveals the observer.

verses.text_hebrew: אָדָ֗ם תּ֭וֹעֶה מִדֶּ֣רֶךְ הַשְׂכֵּ֑ל בִּקְהַ֖ל רְפָאִ֣ים יָנֽוּחַ׃
verses.text_kjv: The man that wandereth out of the way of understanding shall remain in the congregation of the dead.
verses.rendering:
A person who strays from the path of insight
will rest in the assembly of the dead.
translator_notes: Repha'im ('the dead, shades') refers to the inhabitants of Sheol — the shadowy underworld of Israelite cosmology. The proverb warns that abandoning wisdom is not a detour but a fatal turn. The verb yanuach ('will rest, will settle') implies permanence — this is not a temporary wandering but a final destination.

key_terms.semantic_range: shades, departed spirits, the dead; inhabitants of the underworld
key_terms.note: The repha'im are the weakened, diminished dead who dwell in Sheol. The word may derive from raphah ('to be weak, to sink down'). In Proverbs, they represent the ultimate destination of folly.

verses.text_hebrew: אִ֣ישׁ מַ֭חְסוֹר אֹהֵ֣ב שִׂמְחָ֑ה אֹהֵ֥ב יַֽ֝יִן וָשֶׁ֗מֶן לֹ֣א יַעֲשִֽׁיר׃
verses.text_kjv: He that loveth pleasure shall be a poor man: he that loveth wine and oil shall not be rich.
verses.rendering:
Whoever loves pleasure will become poor;
whoever loves wine and oil will never grow rich.
translator_notes: Wine and oil represent the consumable luxuries of Israelite life. The proverb does not condemn enjoyment but warns against making enjoyment the primary pursuit. Ahev ('loves, is devoted to') implies ongoing, habitual orientation — not occasional celebration but lifestyle hedonism.

verses.text_hebrew: כֹּ֣פֶר לַצַּדִּ֣יק רָשָׁ֑ע וְתַ֖חַת יְשָׁרִ֣ים בּוֹגֵֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: The wicked shall be a ransom for the righteous, and the transgressor for the upright.
verses.rendering:
The wicked becomes a ransom for the righteous,
and the treacherous takes the place of the upright.
verses.expanded_rendering: The word kofer ('ransom, substitute, atonement price') is striking: it normally describes the payment that frees a person from death or obligation. Here the wicked person himself becomes the ransom — the trouble intended for the righteous falls on the wicked instead. This is not substitutionary theology but reversal theology: God redirects consequences so that the schemer falls into his own pit. The Haman-Mordecai narrative in Esther is the classic illustration of this principle.
translator_notes: Kofer is the same word used for the ransom price of a life in Exodus 21:30. The proverb appropriates sacrificial-legal language to describe the observed reversal of fortunes: the wicked pay the price meant for the righteous.

key_terms.semantic_range: ransom, substitute, atonement price, bribe; from kaphar ('to cover, to atone')
key_terms.note: In its root sense, kofer is a covering price — what is paid to cover an obligation or avert a penalty. Here the wicked inadvertently pay what the righteous would have owed.

verses.text_hebrew: ט֗וֹב שֶׁ֥בֶת בְּאֶֽרֶץ־מִדְבָּ֑ר מֵאֵ֖שֶׁת מִדְיָנִ֣ים וָכָֽעַס׃
verses.text_kjv: It is better to dwell in the wilderness, than with a contentious and an angry woman.
verses.rendering:
Better to live in a desert land
than with a quarrelsome and irritable woman.
translator_notes: This is the second 'better...than' proverb about domestic strife in this chapter (see v9). The escalation from 'corner of the roof' to 'desert' adds rhetorical force: if a rooftop was insufficient escape, try the wilderness. The addition of va-ka'as ('and anger, and irritation') intensifies the portrait beyond v9. These proverbs are observations about the destructive power of chronic conflict, addressed to the male audience of the collection.

verses.text_hebrew: אוֹצָ֤ר ׀ נֶחְמָ֣ד וָ֭שֶׁמֶן בִּנְוֵ֣ה חָכָ֑ם וּכְסִ֖יל אָדָ֣ם יְבַלְּעֶֽנּוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: There is treasure to be desired and oil in the dwelling of the wise; but a foolish man spendeth it up.
verses.rendering:
Precious treasure and oil fill the home of the wise,
but a fool swallows them up.
translator_notes: The verb yevall'ennu ('swallows it, devours it, consumes it') suggests the fool does not spend resources but gulps them down — an image of wasteful, animal-like consumption. The wise person accumulates because of self-discipline; the fool consumes because he cannot restrain appetite.

verses.text_hebrew: רֹ֭דֵף צְדָקָ֣ה וָחָ֑סֶד יִמְצָ֥א חַ֝יִּ֗ים צְדָקָ֥ה וְכָבֽוֹד׃
verses.text_kjv: He that followeth after righteousness and mercy findeth life, righteousness, and honour.
verses.rendering:
Whoever pursues righteousness and faithful love
finds life, righteousness, and honor.
verses.expanded_rendering: The pursuit of tsedaqah (righteousness) and chesed (faithful love) produces a return that exceeds the investment: not only does the pursuer find life (chayyim) and honor (kavod), but he finds tsedaqah itself — the very thing he pursued is given back to him in deeper measure. This is the paradox of covenant ethics: those who practice righteousness receive righteousness. The life described here is not mere survival but flourishing in every dimension.
translator_notes: The repetition of tsedaqah — once as what is pursued and again as what is found — is deliberate. Righteousness compounds: practicing it produces more of it.

key_terms.semantic_range: faithful love, loyal kindness, covenant devotion, steadfast love, mercy
key_terms.note: Chesed is covenant loyalty expressed as active kindness. It is love that keeps showing up because of commitment, not feeling. Here paired with tsedaqah, it represents the full ethical life.

verses.text_hebrew: עִ֣יר גִּ֭בֹּרִים עָלָ֣ה חָכָ֑ם וַ֝יֹּ֗רֶד עֹ֣ז מִבְטֶחָֽהּ׃
verses.text_kjv: A wise man scaleth the city of the mighty, and casteth down the strength of the confidence thereof.
verses.rendering:
A wise person can scale a city of warriors
and bring down the stronghold they trusted in.
translator_notes: The proverb asserts that wisdom is superior to military strength. A single wise person can accomplish what an army cannot — breach the defenses of the mighty. The verb yarad ('brings down, causes to descend') and oz mivtechah ('the strength of its confidence, the stronghold it trusted') emphasize that wisdom dismantles not just walls but the confidence those walls inspired.

verses.text_hebrew: שֹׁמֵ֣ר פִּ֭יו וּלְשׁוֹנ֑וֹ שֹׁמֵ֖ר מִצָּר֣וֹת נַפְשֽׁוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Whoso keepeth his mouth and his tongue keepeth his soul from troubles.
verses.rendering:
Whoever guards his mouth and his tongue
guards his life from troubles.
translator_notes: The repetition of shomer ('guards, keeps, watches over') creates a direct equation: guarding the mouth equals guarding the life. The nefesh ('life, self, being') is at stake in every word spoken. In Proverbs, speech is never merely verbal — it is existential.

verses.text_hebrew: זֵ֣ד יָ֭הִיר לֵ֣ץ שְׁמ֑וֹ ע֝וֹשֶׂ֗ה בְּעֶבְרַ֥ת זָדֽוֹן׃
verses.text_kjv: Proud and haughty scorner is his name, who dealeth in proud wrath.
verses.rendering:
The proud and arrogant — 'Scoffer' is his name —
he acts with overflowing contempt.
translator_notes: This is a character definition: three words (zed, yahir, lets) pile up to create a complete portrait. Zed ('presumptuous, insolent'), yahir ('haughty, arrogant'), and lets ('scoffer, mocker') converge on a single personality type. The phrase 'Scoffer is his name' uses the naming formula to say: this is not behavior but identity. The 'overflow of presumption' (evrat zadon) suggests someone whose arrogance spills over into action — he cannot contain it.

key_terms.semantic_range: scoffer, mocker, scorner; one who treats serious things with contempt
key_terms.note: The lets is the most dangerous character type in Proverbs — worse than the fool (kesil) because the scoffer actively resists correction. He does not merely lack wisdom; he despises it.

verses.text_hebrew: תַּאֲוַ֣ת עָצֵ֣ל תְּמִיתֶ֑נּוּ כִּֽי־מֵאֲנ֖וּ יָדָ֣יו לַעֲשֽׂוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: The desire of the slothful killeth him; for his hands refuse to labour.
verses.rendering:
The craving of the lazy person kills him,
because his hands refuse to work.
translator_notes: The paradox: the lazy person is not free from desire — he wants as much as anyone. But his hands refuse (me'anu, 'they refuse, they are unwilling') to do the work that would satisfy the desire. He is killed by the gap between wanting and doing. The verb temitenu ('kills him') is not metaphorical — Proverbs treats chronic laziness as self-destruction.

verses.text_hebrew: כָּל־הַ֭יּוֹם הִתְאַוָּ֣ה תַאֲוָ֑ה וְצַדִּ֥יק יִ֝תֵּ֗ן וְלֹ֣א יַחְשֹֽׂךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: He coveteth greedily all the day long: but the righteous giveth and spareth not.
verses.rendering:
All day long he craves and craves,
but the righteous gives without holding back.
translator_notes: The contrast between the lazy craver and the righteous giver completes the portrait begun in v25. The lazy person's entire day is consumed by unsatisfied desire; the righteous person's life overflows in generosity. The verb yachsokh ('withholds, holds back, spares') with the negation means the righteous person gives freely, without calculating.

verses.text_hebrew: זֶ֣בַח רְ֭שָׁעִים תּוֹעֵבָ֑ה אַ֝֗ף כִּֽי־בְזִמָּ֥ה יְבִיאֶֽנּוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: The sacrifice of the wicked is abomination: how much more, when he bringeth it with a wicked mind?
verses.rendering:
The sacrifice of the wicked is detestable —
how much more when offered with evil intent!
translator_notes: The 'how much more' (af ki) construction creates two levels of offense: a wicked person's sacrifice is already to'evah ('detestable, abominable') because his life contradicts his offering; if he also brings it with zimmah ('evil intent, scheming, wickedness') — perhaps to manipulate God or create a pious appearance — the offense compounds. This extends the logic of v3: God prefers justice over sacrifice, and a wicked person's sacrifice is worse than no sacrifice at all.

key_terms.semantic_range: abomination, detestable thing, loathsome act; something that violates the moral order
key_terms.note: To'evah appears frequently in Proverbs for things God finds repulsive — dishonest scales, lying lips, and here, the sacrifice of the wicked. It marks the boundary between what is tolerable and what is absolutely rejected.

verses.text_hebrew: עֵד־כְּזָבִ֥ים יֹאבֵ֑ד וְאִ֥ישׁ שׁ֝וֹמֵ֗עַ לָנֶ֥צַח יְדַבֵּֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: A false witness shall perish: but the man that heareth speaketh constantly.
verses.rendering:
A lying witness will perish,
but the person who listens will speak with lasting authority.
translator_notes: The contrast is between the false witness who invents testimony and the person who shomea ('listens, hears, pays attention') before speaking. The liar's words die with him; the listener's words endure la-netsach ('forever, perpetually, with permanence'). Truth has staying power; lies self-destruct.

verses.text_hebrew: הֵעֵ֣ז אִ֣ישׁ רָשָׁ֣ע בְּפָנָ֑יו וְ֝יָשָׁ֗ר ה֤וּא ׀ יָבִ֬ין דַּרְכּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: A wicked man hardeneth his face: but as for the upright, he directeth his way.
verses.rendering:
A wicked person puts on a bold face,
but the upright considers his way carefully.
translator_notes: He'ez panav ('he makes his face strong, he hardens his countenance') describes brazen, shameless posturing — the wicked person projects confidence he does not have. The upright person, by contrast, yavin darkho ('understands his way, discerns his path') — he thinks before acting. The contrast is between performed certainty and genuine discernment.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֣ין חָ֭כְמָה וְאֵ֣ין תְּבוּנָ֑ה וְאֵ֥ין עֵ֝צָ֗ה לְנֶ֣גֶד יְהוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: There is no wisdom nor understanding nor counsel against the LORD.
verses.rendering:
There is no wisdom, no understanding,
no counsel that can stand against the LORD.
verses.expanded_rendering: Three negations — ein chokhmah, ein tevunah, ein etsah — systematically eliminate every form of human intellectual achievement as a rival to God. Wisdom (chokhmah), understanding (tevunah), and counsel (etsah) represent the complete range of human thinking: insight, comprehension, and strategic planning. None of them can succeed le-neged ('against, in opposition to, before the face of') the LORD. The proverb does not devalue wisdom — the entire book promotes it — but it subordinates all human wisdom to divine sovereignty. This is the theological ceiling of the wisdom tradition: pursue wisdom with everything you have, but never imagine it gives you leverage over God.
translator_notes: The triple ein ('there is not') is emphatic and total. No exceptions, no qualifications. Human wisdom is real and valuable — but it is never ultimate.

verses.text_hebrew: ס֗וּס מ֭וּכָן לְי֣וֹם מִלְחָמָ֑ה וְ֝לַֽיהוָ֗ה הַתְּשׁוּעָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: The horse is prepared against the day of battle: but safety is of the LORD.
verses.rendering:
The horse is prepared for the day of battle,
but victory belongs to the LORD.
verses.expanded_rendering: The chapter's concluding proverb is a masterpiece of compressed theology. The horse — the most advanced military technology of the ancient world — represents the maximum of human preparation. The proverb does not say 'do not prepare the horse.' Prepare it. Train it. But know this: ha-teshu'ah ('the deliverance, the victory, the salvation') belongs to the LORD. Human effort and divine sovereignty are not alternatives; they are layers. You prepare; God delivers. The definite article on teshu'ah is significant: it is not 'a victory' but 'the victory' — ultimate outcome belongs to God categorically.
translator_notes: Teshu'ah (from yasha, 'to save, to deliver') is the same root as the name Yehoshua (Joshua) and Yeshua (Jesus). The word encompasses military victory, rescue from danger, and spiritual salvation. Here it primarily means the outcome of battle, but the theological resonance runs deeper.

key_terms.semantic_range: salvation, deliverance, victory, rescue; the act of being saved from danger or defeat
key_terms.note: From yasha ('to save'). In military contexts, teshu'ah means victory; in personal contexts, rescue; in theological contexts, salvation. The word always points to an outcome that comes from outside the person's own power.
